_comment: The evaluation protocol, frozen as data.
quark's perplexity is only comparable to GPT-2's if both are measured the
same way, and the two are measured by different programs in different
languages -- src/eval/corpus.rs in Rust, experiments/gpt2_baseline.py in
Python. This file is what they agree on. Both assert against it:
* Rust: `cargo test the_frozen_protocol` (matches all four sections)
* Python: `python experiments/gpt2_baseline.py --self-test`
If either implementation drifts, one of those two fails. Without this the
drift would show up as a perplexity difference and be attributed to the
model, which is exactly the mistake the whole comparison exists to avoid.
See docs/DESIGN.md §3 and §3.1.
A window starting at `start` reads tokens[start .. start+seq_len+1]:
seq_len inputs and one extra token, the target of the last input. Its
position t predicts tokens[start+t+1]. Positions before `score_from` were
already scored by the previous window and are masked out, so overlapping
windows score each token exactly once.
n_windows = 0 if n_tokens < seq_len+1, else (n_tokens - seq_len - 1)/stride + 1,
integer division. The final partial window is dropped, not padded.
n_scored = sum over windows of (seq_len - score_from).

cases.name: disjoint windows tiling the corpus exactly
cases._note: 12 == n_tokens - 1: every token but the first, which nothing precedes.

cases.name: disjoint windows with a dropped tail
cases._note: Token 13 is unreachable: a fourth window would need tokens 12..17. Coverage 12/13. The shortfall lowers total NLL, i.e. it flatters us, so it is reported rather than absorbed.

cases.name: half-overlapping windows: every token gets >= stride of context
cases._note: Same 12 tokens as the disjoint case, each scored once -- but with more context, so a lower NLL. This is why a strided number must never be compared to an unstrided one.

cases.name: a corpus barely longer than one window
cases._note: Coverage 8/9. Small corpora lose a large fraction; this is the case that makes coverage() worth printing.

cases.name: a corpus too short for any window
cases._note: Needs seq_len+1 = 9 tokens. Padding to reach it would score tokens the corpus does not contain.

_comment: How a corpus file becomes the token stream that gets scored. Rust:
split_wikitext_articles and prepare_shard (src/data/mod.rs) plus
ShardWriter::push_document (src/data/shard.rs). Python: split_wikitext_articles,
documents, corpus_denominators, build_stream.
This section exists because of a bug it would have caught. The two
implementations agreed on every counting function below and still measured
different things: Rust split WikiText into articles and summed the
denominators per document, while Python tokenized the whole file as one
stream. Same counters, different text, and no test could see it -- the
'denominators' section pins how to count, and that is not the same question
as what to count.
split_rule: WikiText writes an article heading as ` = Title = ` and a section heading as
` = = Section = = `, so an article starts at a trimmed line that begins with
'= ', ends with ' =', and does NOT begin with '= = '. Lines are split on '\n'
only -- not on \v, \f, \x1c or \u2028, which Python's splitlines() would also
break on and Rust's split_inclusive('\n') would not.
Each document is trimmed; empty ones are dropped. Splitting therefore
preserves every word but NOT every byte, which is the whole point of the

cases.name: article headings split, section headings do not
cases.text:
= Valkyria Chronicles III = 

 Senjo no Valkyria 3 is a game .

 = = Gameplay = = 

 It is a tactical RPG .

 = Tower Building = 

 The Tower Building of the Little Rock Arsenal .
documents:
= Valkyria Chronicles III = 

 Senjo no Valkyria 3 is a game .

 = = Gameplay = = 

 It is a tactical RPG .

= Tower Building = 

 The Tower Building of the Little Rock Arsenal .
cases._note: The two byte counts differ -- 176 vs 182 -- because trimming discards the whitespace around each article. The word counts do not. That asymmetry is exactly why bits-per-byte must be summed per document: counting it on the whole file would divide quark and GPT-2 by different numbers and report the difference as a result.

cases.name: text without an article heading is one document
cases.text:
just some prose
over two lines
documents:
just some prose
over two lines
cases._note: One document, and still a byte short of the file: the trailing newline belongs to no document.

_comment: Each document's tokens, then one EOS separator. The separator is ours, not
the corpus's: the model must predict it, so it is scored, but it counts
toward neither denominator. The id differs between the models -- 0 for
quark, 50256 (<|endoftext|>) for GPT-2 -- because the role is what is
shared, not the number.
The numerator of word perplexity is model-specific; the denominator is a
property of the text alone, and must be identical for every model or the
comparison is meaningless. Both are counted on the *source text*, before
tokenization and before our own EOS separators are inserted.
This pins how to count. 'document_stream' above pins what to count -- the
documents, not the file. Agreeing on one and not the other is not agreement.
denominators.words: text.split_whitespace().count() in Rust == len(text.split()) in Python. Deliberately crude: it is the definition the published WikiText-103 counts use.
denominators.bytes: len(text.encode('utf-8')). Rust's str::len() is already this.
denominators.eos_separators: Counted as tokens (the model must predict them) but toward neither denominator (the corpus never contained them).

cases._note: 2 words, but 12 bytes for 10 chars: the accented letters are 2 bytes each. Bits-per-byte counts bytes.

_comment: BLiMP has no citable GPT-2-small number (docs/DESIGN.md §3.2), so we measure
both models ourselves -- quark in src/eval/blimp.rs, GPT-2 in
experiments/gpt2_baseline.py --blimp. Two programs again, so the same
argument applies: what could silently differ is pinned here.
Scoring is BLiMP's own simple_LM_method: the sum of log P(token | bos, prefix)
over every token of the sentence, unnormalized by length. Not pinned as data
because it is the model's own numerator -- the parts below are not.
A pair is correct iff the grammatical sentence scores strictly higher.
Ties are WRONG. This is not a rounding detail: a uniform model ties on
every equal-length pair, and scoring ties as correct would report it at
near 100% -- the one rule that makes a broken model look perfect.
Rust: eval::blimp::is_correct. Python: is_correct.

cases.name: exact tie is wrong, not a coin flip

_comment: Pair-weighted, not paradigm-weighted. The full release has 1000 pairs in
every paradigm so the two agree there; they diverge on any subset, and
pair-weighting is the one that keeps meaning what it says.
aggregation._note: 550/1010 = 54.46%. Paradigm-weighted would say 80.00% -- 25 points higher, on the strength of a 10-pair paradigm counting as much as a 900-pair one.

formulas._comment: Computed from a single summed NLL in nats. Never averaged from per-batch averages: exp(mean of means) != exp(total/total) unless every batch is the same size, and the last one never is.
formulas.token_ppl: exp(total_nll / n_scored_tokens) -- tokenizer-dependent, never compared across models
